explicit_text: As a child, my parents gave me a stack of classical 45s, and among them was the William Tell Overture. I used to build blocks while listening to those classical marches when I was about five years old. That early exposure to classical music like the William Tell Overture helped spark my lifelong interest in music.

explicit_text: When Elvis Presley came out, I got really into his music and started singing his songs. My mom always told me I'd do something with my voice someday, and singing Elvis songs was an early part of that journey. It was during my elementary and high school years, especially in Catholic school, that singing became a big part of my life.

explicit_text: I went to Catholic school, and singing was a big part of the church experience and school life. We sang all the time, whether in church or at football games, so music was deeply embedded in my daily routine. That environment really nurtured my love for singing from a young age.

explicit_text: In the 1970s, I was part of a group called the Love Family, which was a big commune located on Queen Anne Hill. It was a vibrant community with lots of guitars and musicians, and that's where I really started learning to play guitar and folk songs. Queen Anne Hill was an important place in my musical development during that time.

explicit_text: I was involved with the Love Family in the 1970s, a large commune on Queen Anne Hill. Being surrounded by many guitar players there helped me learn how to play guitar and pick up old-time folk songs. It was also where I started collecting folk material and teaching songs to kids.

explicit_text: I was in a choir where we learned Italian opera chants, which are vocal exercises using the five vowels. These chants helped with breath control and tone matching, and I started using them with children as well. I still use those Italian opera chants today for teaching young kids how to sing.

explicit_text: My daughter joined the Seattle Girls Choir, and at one point, their director wanted to see what I did with young kids. He was amazed to see three-year-olds doing Italian opera chants and other vocal exercises. That led to me working with the Seattle Girls Choir, doing some classes for them.

explicit_text: Bill Craig was the musician who taught me those Italian opera chants back in the 1970s. He's now a composer in the Bay Area, and I recently contacted him to tell him I still use those chants with kids. He was surprised to hear that, as he learned them in college and didn't know anyone still used them.

explicit_text: In my Montessori training, I learned about Maria Montessori's approach of whittling ideas down to their core concepts. That philosophy influenced how I teach voice to children, simplifying it to just sounds and facial expressions to help form the larynx correctly. Maria Montessori's educational principles have deeply shaped my teaching methods.

This approach has been central to my work with children’s vocal education.
